items.question_text: When may a takeoff clearance be issued before the prescribed separation already exists?

choices.text: When there is reasonable assurance the separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll.
Whenever the pilot reports ready and no wake turbulence interval applies.
Only after the preceding aircraft passes the runway end.
Only if the next aircraft accepts reduced separation.

items.explanation: Takeoff clearance need not be withheld until separation actually exists if there is reasonable assurance it will exist when takeoff roll begins.

items.question_text: If prescribed separation does not yet exist but you have reasonable assurance it will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll, you may issue the takeoff clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the rule in 3-9-5.

items.instruction: Which factor allows you to anticipate separation for takeoff?
items.question_text: Which factor allows anticipation?

choices.text: Reasonable assurance that the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.
A pilot statement that separation looks adequate from the cockpit.
The next aircraft having a shorter taxi route than the preceding aircraft.
Any VFR condition with both aircraft in sight.

items.explanation: The permission to anticipate separation is based on reasonable assurance that the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.

items.question_text: Use takeoff clearance anticipation only when there is reasonable assurance the prescribed separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The controller may anticipate separation, but only on that reasonable-assurance basis.

items.question_text: What is the basic same-runway separation rule for a departing aircraft behind another aircraft using the same runway?

choices.text: Do not let it begin takeoff roll until the required same-runway or wake interval condition exists.
Let it roll as soon as the preceding aircraft is airborne, regardless of category or runway position.
Let it roll once the preceding arrival reports braking action good.
Let it roll whenever the pilot requests full length.

items.explanation: Same-runway separation is achieved by delaying takeoff roll until the required departure, arrival-clear, or interval condition exists.

items.instruction: Which case allows visual separation instead of using the distance minima in 3-9-6a?
items.question_text: Which case allows visual separation?

choices.text: When the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.
When either aircraft is Category III.
When the preceding aircraft is clear of the runway.
When the runway is at least 10,000 feet long.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifically allows visual separation in lieu of the distance minima when the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

items.instruction: Is this same-runway distance rule correct?
items.question_text: If you can determine distances by reference to suitable landmarks and only Category I aircraft are involved, the preceding aircraft needs only to be airborne if at least 3,000 feet exists between the aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The Category I airborne-distance minimum in that case is 3,000 feet.

items.instruction: Is this Category E wake rule correct?
items.question_text: If a Category I aircraft departs behind a Category E aircraft from the same runway or a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet, you must separate them by 2 minutes.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That 2-minute interval applies to a Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft on the same runway or a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet.

items.instruction: One word in this wake instruction is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: The approved phraseology in 3-9-6 is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.situation: A departure must wait because of wake turbulence from previous traffic on the same runway. Build the instruction.

A Category I aircraft is ready to depart behind a Category E aircraft. The operations will use the same runway. The pilot asks to waive the interval because the Category E aircraft is already airborne and easy to see. Another controller says visual contact is enough and the waiver can be approved to keep the line moving.
items.para_context: A Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft must be separated by 2 minutes in the listed same-runway and parallel-runway cases. Pilot requests to deviate from the intervals in subparagraphs f through k must not be approved.

choices.text: Apply the 2-minute interval and do not approve the pilot's request to deviate from it.
Approve the waiver because the aircraft is airborne and visible, which replaces the Category E interval automatically.
Use the Category I 3,000-foot airborne distance instead of the Category E interval because the succeeding aircraft is also Category I.
Issue takeoff clearance immediately because wake-turbulence intervals apply only to arriving aircraft.

items.explanation: The Category E interval still applies, and the paragraph expressly forbids approving requests to deviate from the required intervals.

items.question_text: How long must a Category I aircraft wait when taking off from an intersection behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft on the same runway?

choices.text: At least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.
At least 2 minutes after the preceding aircraft starts takeoff roll.
Until the preceding aircraft is 3,000 feet ahead.
Only until the preceding aircraft crosses the runway end.

items.explanation: That is the stated interval for a Category I intersection departure behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft.

items.instruction: Which operation is treated as departing from an intersection for wake-turbulence purposes?
items.question_text: Which operation is treated as an intersection departure?

choices.text: A touch-and-go or stop-and-go operation.
A low approach that stays over the centerline.
A full-stop landing that exits at the end of the runway.
A taxi back for departure from the full length.

items.explanation: The note says aircraft conducting touch-and-go and stop-and-go operations are considered to be departing from an intersection.

items.instruction: One word in this wake instruction is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: The approved phraseology in 3-9-7 is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.instruction: Is this Category E intersection rule correct?
items.question_text: If a Category I aircraft departs from an intersection behind a departing Category E aircraft, you must wait at least 3 minutes if the Category E aircraft departed from the same runway, from a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet, or from a parallel runway separated by 700 feet or more when projected flight paths will cross.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the listed Category E cases for the 3-minute interval.

items.instruction: Is this hold instruction directive correct?
items.question_text: Inform aircraft when it is necessary to hold in order to provide the required wake-turbulence time interval.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph specifically says to inform aircraft when it is necessary to hold in order to provide the required interval.

items.situation: An intersection departure must wait because wake-turbulence separation is required. Build the instruction.

A touch-and-go aircraft will depart again from an intersection behind a preceding aircraft that requires wake-turbulence separation. The pilot says they will maintain visual spacing behind the larger aircraft. Another controller says that works even if the lead aircraft is Category A because the pilot volunteered to keep the spacing visually.
items.para_context: Touch-and-go and stop-and-go operations are treated as departing from an intersection. Successive touch-and-go or stop-and-go operations may use pilot-maintained visual separation or spacing behind the preceding larger aircraft only in the listed cases, and the procedure is not authorized with a Category A aircraft as the lead or departure aircraft.

choices.text: Do not use that visual-spacing exception with a Category A lead aircraft; the note says it is not authorized.
Allow it because any pilot-maintained visual spacing automatically replaces the wake interval for touch-and-go operations.
Allow it only if the pilot also accepts reduced runway separation in writing.
Treat the touch-and-go as a normal full-length departure instead of an intersection departure and ignore the wake rule.

items.explanation: The note permits the visual-spacing exception only in listed cases and specifically says it is not authorized with a Category A lead or departure aircraft.

items.question_text: When may a takeoff clearance be issued before the prescribed separation already exists?
items.explanation: The clearance may be issued when there is reasonable assurance the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.

choices.text: When there is reasonable assurance the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.
Only after the required separation fully exists.
Only after the pilot requests anticipated separation.
Only in VFR conditions.

items.question_text: If you have reasonable assurance the required separation will exist when takeoff roll begins, you may anticipate separation and issue the takeoff clearance.
items.explanation: That is the full rule in 3-9-5.

items.question_text: What must be true before anticipated separation is used for takeoff?
items.explanation: The required element is reasonable assurance that the prescribed separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll.

choices.text: Reasonable assurance that the prescribed separation will exist when takeoff roll begins.
Pilot acceptance of reduced separation.
The preceding aircraft already being past the runway end.
The succeeding aircraft being Category I only.

items.question_text: Anticipated separation allows a takeoff clearance to be issued simply because the controller expects the pilot to delay takeoff roll voluntarily.
items.explanation: The rule is based on reasonable assurance that the required separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll, not on assuming a pilot will create the delay.

items.question_text: When only Category I aircraft are involved and you can determine distances by reference to suitable landmarks, what minimum airborne distance applies before the succeeding aircraft begins takeoff roll?
items.explanation: The minimum is 3,000 feet.

choices.text: 3,000 feet.
4,500 feet.
6,000 feet.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: Which aircraft category definition is correct for same-runway separation?
items.explanation: Category II is defined as small twin-engine propeller-driven aircraft weighing 12,500 pounds or less.

choices.text: Category II is small twin-engine propeller-driven aircraft weighing 12,500 pounds or less.
Category II is all helicopters and small single-engine propeller aircraft.
Category II is all aircraft other than small propeller aircraft.
Category II is all Heavy aircraft.

items.question_text: When may visual separation be applied in lieu of the distance minima in 3-9-6a?
items.explanation: It may be used when the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

choices.text: When the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.
When the preceding aircraft is Category III.
When both aircraft are Category II.
When both pilots report traffic in sight.

items.question_text: A Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft on the same runway or a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet must be separated by 2 minutes.
items.explanation: That is the Category E interval in 3-9-6g.

items.question_text: What phraseology is used when an aircraft must wait for the required wake interval?
items.explanation: The phraseology is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

choices.text: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.
WAIT FOR DEPARTURE SPACING.
CONTINUE HOLDING FOR TRAFFIC INTERVAL.
HOLD FOR SAME RUNWAY CLEARANCE.

items.question_text: May pilot requests to deviate from the required intervals in subparagraphs f through k be approved?
items.explanation: 3-9-6l says they must not be approved.

choices.text: No.
Yes, if the pilot accepts wake responsibility.
Yes, in VFR only.
Yes, if the succeeding aircraft is Category I.

items.question_text: How long must a Category I aircraft wait when departing from an intersection behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft on the same runway?
items.explanation: The interval is at least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.

choices.text: At least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.
At least 2 minutes after the preceding aircraft starts takeoff roll.
Until 3,000 feet of airborne distance exists.
Until the preceding aircraft is clear of the runway.

items.question_text: A Category I aircraft behind a departing Category E aircraft must wait at least 3 minutes if the Category E aircraft departed from the same runway or from a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet.
items.explanation: That is part of the Category E interval rule in 3-9-7a2.

items.question_text: For intersection departures on the same runway or parallels separated by less than 2,500 feet, what interval applies to Categories B through I behind a Category A aircraft?
items.explanation: The interval is 4 minutes.

choices.text: 4 minutes.
3 minutes.
2 minutes.
6 minutes.

items.question_text: Which operations are considered to be departing from an intersection for wake-turbulence purposes?
items.explanation: Touch-and-go and stop-and-go operations are treated as departing from an intersection.

choices.text: Touch-and-go and stop-and-go operations.
Low approaches only.
Full-stop landings followed by taxi back only.
Any departure cleared for the option.

items.question_text: What phraseology is used when an intersection departure must wait for wake turbulence?
items.explanation: The phraseology is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

choices.text: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.
WAIT FOR INTERSECTION DEPARTURE INTERVAL.
CONTINUE HOLDING FOR DEPARTURE SPACING.
HOLD FOR RUNWAY OCCUPANCY.

items.question_text: Is the successive touch-and-go or stop-and-go visual-spacing exception authorized with a Category A lead or departure aircraft?
items.explanation: The note says it is not authorized with a Category A aircraft as the lead or departure aircraft.

choices.text: No.
Yes, if the pilot volunteers to maintain visual spacing.
Yes, during daylight only.
Yes, if the runways are parallel and more than 2,500 feet apart.
